topic: Slices - split_at_mut for Disjoint Mutable Borrows

turns.content: I'm trying to understand why I can't do this with my vector. What's the difference between calling split_at_mut twice versus just calling it once?

Great question. The core issue is that split_at_mut returns two separate &mut slices, but Rust's borrow checker enforces a single mutable reference to the original Vec at any given moment. When you call split_at_mut once, it produces two non-overlapping &mut [T] views of the same underlying data without needing an extra borrow. If you try calling split_at_mut twice in sequence on the same variable, you're attempting to create two distinct mutable borrows simultaneously, which violates the ownership rules.
That sounds obvious, but I'm still stuck. Why does it reject even when I assign both results to different variables? Is it just a limitation of my compiler version?

It's not a compiler version issue; this is fundamental to how mutable borrows work in Rust. The borrow checker ensures that you never have two active &mut references pointing to the same data structure at once, which prevents data races and accidental overwrites. Even if your variables are different, they both ultimately reference the same Vec instance, so the total number of simultaneous mutable borrows is still limited to one.
Okay, but what about split_at_mut_unchecked? Can I use that to bypass this restriction?

You can use split_at_mut_unchecked if you're absolutely sure the indices are valid and there's no overlap, but it requires an explicit unsafe block. This is useful when you've already computed your split points in a way that guarantees safety without needing the borrow checker to verify it for you.
